title: [Vlog Korean] What Locals REALLY Say in Korea's Historical Village📍🚗

cues.text: Hello. / Hello. Where to?
Eunpyeong Hanok Village, please. / Okay.
How many minutes is it from here? / Probably not longer than 10 minutes.
Ah, okay.
I heard there’s a temple next to it, Jingwansa. I’m going there too.
Right, there is.
Today’s the Suneung, and when I was there earlier there were two groups praying.
Ah, they go to pray for students taking the exam?
One had two grandchildren taking the exam,
and the other said her daughter was taking it.
It’s today, right? / Yes.
Here. / Tap it there.
Thank you.
Thank you. / Have a good day.
And one shrimp pilaf, please. / Shrimp pilaf.
No drink today?
There’s actually one food that’s my favorite.
Thank you, King Sejong. King Sejong, you saved my life.
Hi. For two adults, please. That’s 2,000 won.
He was so handsome.
She called me handsome. Thank you.
Where are you from?
Poland. / Ah, Poland.
Mountains vs. the sea. 1, 2, 3!
Sea! / Mountains.
What’s your favorite season?
Summer! / Autumn!
We’re not very compatible. / We’re not compatible. We aren’t.
Hello. It’s Yoo Seung-wan from Talk To Me In Korean.
Today I’m at Eunpyeong Hanok Village.
Hanok are traditional Korean houses.
And this is a village filled with them.
Today I’m not going to walk around alone, but film with another Korean teacher.
A lot of our videos are just me talking alone.
I thought this way you can hear more ways of speaking and more conversation,
so I invited another teacher.
There he is.
Hi. How have you been? / How are you?
Good to see you.
Hello, I’m Spixy Korean. Lee Gwan-hee.
Shall we eat something simple and look around? / Sure, sure.
Okay.
Hello.
The menu is here in front. Take your time. I’ll take your order on the first floor.
What’s this? Egg in Hell?
What’s Egg in Hell?
Egg in Hell is poached eggs with bread dipped in tomato sauce.
What you see in the image is eggs benedict. It’s served like a hamburger.
One bacon egg panini. / One bacon egg panini.
And one shrimp pilaf, please. / Shrimp pilaf.
No drink today? / Are you ok with no drink?
We’ll probably go somewhere to drink coffee later, so.
If that’s all, I’ll take payment now. / Okay.
Please insert your card.
Payment is complete. Here’s your beeper.
We have two floors. Everything you need is on the second floor.
Thank you. . Thank you.
What a great view.
You can really see the difference with the autumn colors.
Where do you want to sit?
Shall we sit here? / Sure.
Is it your first time here, or…?/ It’s my first time.
Ah, really? / Today is completely my first time.
It’s my first time, too. / I had only heard about it.
I’ll go. / Let’s go together.
Okay./ Enjoy your food. The first floor…
Is there water on the second floor? / Yes.
Yes. / okay.
Enjoy. Please return your dishes on the side here.
Thank you./ Enjoy your food.
I’ll take the water and cutlery. / Okay.
Thank you for the food. / No problem.
Is this a mukbang? / Yes, it’s a mukbang.
It’s just an egg. How is it so sweet?
What in the world did they do?
The bread is really soft. / You’re right.
How long did it take to get here, by the way?
An hour and 10 minutes? An hour to an hour and 10 minutes.
The forecast said it might rain, so I was a bit worried, honestly.
Good thing it didn’t. / Really good thing.
It doesn’t look like rain weather, to be honest. It’s just cloudy. It won’t rain.
Do you have a favorite food?
There’s actually one food that’s my favorite.
Just one?
Like, if I rank foods, it’s not “This is good and so is this,” but “This is number one.”
There’s just number one. Number one.
Should I take a guess? / Sure. Try to guess. What is it?
Just tell me what category it is.
Korean, Chinese, Japanese, or western.
It’s Japanese food. / Japanese food?
Sushi!
Correct.
I go crazy for it. There’s never enough.
Thank you. / Goodbye.
Thank you. Goodbye. / Goodbye.
Before we start looking around, the Hanok Museum is just next door.
Let’s go to the Hanok Museum!
It’s so peaceful and nice. Especially since it’s Thursday.
A Friday or Saturday would have a lot of people. But it’s Thursday, so this is great.
Look at this. This is…geez, I want to live in one of these.
The ticket office. 매표소.
Hi. For two adults, please. That’s 2,000 won.
Wow, that’s so cheap.
Please insert your ticket.
Wait, no one checks your ticket? You just put it in?
Ta-da.
We put it in. We came in legally.
It’s really done like a hanok. / They made the entrance really nice, too.
Holy. Look at this setup. Look.
Hanok, nobility. This is where sons of nobility can study.
It looks like really uncomfortable, though.
Now Koreans like sofas better than sitting on the floor, guys.
Don’t get me wrong. I like sofas too. / That’s true.
In the past they had to memorize all of this. These hanja.
They had to memorize hanja, Chinese characters.
Newspapers used hanja up until even the 90s. / They did.
With hangeul, it’s much easier.
Thank you so much, King Sejong.
Thank you, King Sejong. King Sejong, you saved my life.
This is perfect for taking pictures. Should I take one for you?
Take one for me.
Your phone looks really nice.
It’s a 15 Pro.
Oh, very nice. You try taking one.
This one turned out great. The first one.
How’s this vibe?
Great. I like it.
We heard there was a viewing point, so we climbed up.
You can see all of the Hanok Village this way.
Back there is the mountain. / You can see Bukhansan, and the autumn colors are out beautifully right now.
Shall we go down and look up close, then?
Sure, let’s go down closer. / Okay.
Have you ever lived in a hanok like this, by the way?
When I was young, I never once lived in an apartment.
I lived in a detached house, and the roof was similar to this. / Yes.
On one side there’s a window, and normally there’s no door on the other side.
But there was a door on the other side, too.
So you could stay cool in the summer if you opened them both.
Ah, really? / Because the wind can pass through.
So, we didn’t have an air conditioner.
I’ve never lived somewhere like that. I’d like to once.
I wonder what they’re filming. / I wonder.
This is something you can’t avoid, but one thing about hanok
is that you can’t use the space above the roof, right?
You should be able to hang your laundry and stuff.
There’s no rooftop. / Right, there’s no rooftop.
Because if there is, it’s not hanok. / If there were, it wouldn’t have that beautiful roof.
This is a residential area. Please talk quietly.
The word 소곤소곤 feels cute all of a sudden, now that I hear it.
It was just “quietly” in English, but 소곤소곤 is cute.
so-gon-so-gon.
Should we go this way? / Over here? There’s a bridge.
Apparently there’s a temple if you go up here, Jingwansa.
So, let’s go there.
Buddhist temples are great. I love them.
You like "me"? (a sudden dad joke) (nod - he didn't catch it)
When foreigners ask me the best time to visit Korea, I say from the second week of April to late May,
and then late September to the second week of November, around there.
That’s very specific. / I’m very exact.
Because you get cherry blossom season in there once, and then there’s the Suneung, right? Once that’s over it gets really cold.
Eunpyeong. 비우다 (to empty).
터. 공간. Place.
I’ll be bac—I’ll go empty myself.
I’ll go empty myself.
Pardon? It’s a camera.
Then how do you...?
This is the lens. / Do you look through this?
Yes, you look at the screen with this, and this is the lens. / Gosh, we’d need a magnifying glass.
But why is it filming this way? / Ah, this turns around when you twist here.
Ah. / It’s like a head.
Wow, this must be great to carry around. / Right, because it’s light.
You can just connect it to your phone, then. Then it’ll show again.
Then it’ll show again. / Exactly.
How is it different from using a phone?
On your phone, if you get a call, you have to stop filming. But this…
You’re so handsome, though. / Me?
Yes. / Really? Thank you.
(I have to get this) I was looking at this and then when I looked here, he was so handsome.
She called me handsome. Thank you.
Who is it?
I forget the name. You look like that celebrity.
I look like a celebrity, in any case, right?
Yay!
Are you coming back from Jingwansa? / Yes.
We’re on our way to Jingwansa, too. / This young man is just as handsome.
You two look alike. You’re similar.
This is so nice. / This camera?
So great to carry around. / Yes, it’s nice.
You can take selfies like this. / Where’s the shutter?
Ah, it’s filming right now. It’s been filming video, though.
Goodness, that’s so (unintelligible) Have a good day. / Goodbye. Thank you.
Wow, and now there are pupp—there are cats.
Things keep happening, one by one.
She must be an exchange student at HUFS (Hankuk University of Foreign Studies).
Hi. I was a student at HUFS.
Ah.
Can you speak Korean?
A little bit. / Ah, really?
Where are you from?
Poland. / Ah, Poland.
The cats are the main attraction.
This must be the entrance to Jingwansa. / We’re at the entrance to Jingwansa.
Mountains vs. the sea. 1, 2, 3!
Sea! / Mountains.
Ah, you prefer the sea?
Because there’s no sea in Jecheon. It’s just mountains there.
Jecheon is way more mountainous than Cheongju or Chungju.
So, everywhere is mountains. Turn around anywhere and it’s mountains.
But there’s no sea, you know? So every summer, I wanted to go to the sea.
You already saw enough mountains as a kid.
Of course! Picnics all the time, picking acorns with friends, all that.
I prefer mountains over the sea, though.
I mean, you can go to the mountains in Seoul.
There are a lot of mountains within Seoul. / There are.
Actually, it’s more like—should I say forest?
I like places with a lot of trees better than the sea.
Is there somewhere you like when it comes to the sea? / Gangneung.
That was fast. Gangneung.
What do you do when you go? / My grandma lives in Gangneung,
so I visit her, and then if you go a bit further by car there’s Gyeongpodae,
the biggest beach on the East Sea.
So I spend a day… / Do you swim?
I’ve never swam, even in summer. / Fishing?
I go for walks there.
The walking paths by the sea are really nice. / You don’t do much related to the sea.
I don’t. I don’t go in. I never go in the water.
I don’t go in. I don’t want to get wet.
I want to hear the sound. / Ah, the sound of the sea, of the waves.
Exactly. / And also, hoe (raw fish).
Hoe. Hoe while looking out.
And an iced americano. That’s as far as I go.
Road. Sidewalk.
보도 is where you walk.
And 차도 is where cars drive.
I like that we’re finding easy Korean. Here’s more.
Try reading this, everyone.
The garden of the heart.
I took a taxi on the way here, and I talked to the driver a bit.
He said a lot of parents were here praying today.
Wait a second. Is today the day of the Suneung?
Yes. Today’s the Suneung. / Ah, that’s why…
Ah, they’re right in the middle of it. / That’s why the older people are here too.
Good luck, everyone.
It’s written here. / Right, it is.
Praying for high points, Suneung 2025.
I like this kind of thing. This mindset.
You are the Buddha. / Don’t seek far away.
You are the Buddha. / Don’t seek far away.
(Empty) There’s no water right now, but it’s nice.
In midsummer when there’s a lot of rain, it must really pour down here. / Yeah.
Why is it so dry, though? / There hasn’t been rain for a while, to be honest.
This autumn it really hasn’t rained.
Looks like they do temple stays here. / You’re right. I’d love to do one.
A temple stay? / Yeah. I’m very interested.
I’ve done a temple stay before. / Really? At which temple?
In Jeollanam-do, Gurye.
Hwaeomsa.
Hwaeomsa. That place is really famous. / I did one there. I liked it.
대웅전. That’s all I can read. I can’t read the rest.
“It is said that if you pray sincerely in Nahanjeon,
one wish will surely come true.” Let’s pray before we go, too.
What did you wish for?
For lots of views on this video.
O God of the Algorithm!
What’s your favorite season?
Summer! / Autumn!
We’re not very compatible. / We’re not compatible. We aren’t.
One thing I love about summer is that crazy feeling.
That summer night feeling that something is about to happen?
That really suits you, though. That vibe. The summer vibe.
Like you’re about to go crazy. And in the autumn, you get more chill, right.
(Very chill) I like autumn because it’s chill.
That suits you.
Wow, the water’s all dried up. This was all water.
The water flowed and people would get in here and swim.
Little kids would play too, but now it’s all dry.
(Trickle trickle) Still, the water is really clear. Look, you can see the bottom. / Right? It’s really clear.
I guess because it’s under a mountain.
Is that how you do it?
No.
Don’t do it like this. Don’t do it.
Equipment you’ll find in every Korean park.
Can you exercise like that?
I mean, it feels good. I don’t know if it counts as exercise.
Feeling good is what’s important. / It feels good, that’s it.
Hanok Village, Jingwansa, and what should we call this here in front?
The Barren Stream. / The Barren Stream, and the park.
It was fun. / It was really fun. I had a great time.
We got away from the craziness of Seoul. This was so quiet and so nice.
Hello. / Hello.
One regular iced Americano for me, please. / And one warm ginger tea.
That will be 12,000 won. / Thank you.
What was this called?
Ginger tea. / Ginger tea.
I like ginger tea. / Ginger tea and iced Americano.
Thank you. / Thank you. Have a good day.
Have a good day.
We’ll end today’s video here, then.
We’ll finish it here.
Please watch Spixy Korean’s YouTube and Instagram.
I’ll be back next time with a fun video somewhere else, and lots of nice videos and conversations
that will help your Korean.
Bye. / Thank you! See you.
